project_overview.project_development_objective: To respond promptly and effectively to an eligible crisis disaster in Somalia.
project_overview.total_financing: Not specified in document (financing drawn from undisbursed balances of host IDA projects via Rapid Response Option, up to 10% of undisbursed balances per fiscal year)
project_overview.implementation_period: June 30, 2025 – June 30, 2031 (6 years; each activation must be completed within 12 months)
project_overview.beneficiaries: National scope; 600,000 to 1.8 million people directly benefitting per activation; focus on vulnerable groups including women, youth, elderly, displaced persons, and people with disabilities

primary_locations: Federal Member States (FMS) – nationwide coverage intended
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes – Somalia is explicitly classified as a Fragile State and Conflict-affected country. The project checkboxes confirm 'Fragile State(s)' and 'Conflict' implementation modalities. Districts across Somalia experience violence and security incidents affecting access and staff safety.

key_components.description: Single component covering all CERP activities upon activation. Includes: (1) Emergency Livelihood Support to Households – scaling cash transfers and cash-for-work programs via the Baxnaano program and Unified Social Registry; (2) Provision of Essential Services/Supplies – procurement and distribution of emergency water and sanitation, health and nutrition, livestock protection, relief supplies, and non-food items. Activities are quick-disbursing, within existing footprint, and designed to minimize environmental and social risks.
key_components.financing: Not specified as a fixed amount; drawn from undisbursed balances of host IDA projects upon activation

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Finance (MoF) – through the existing Somalia Enhancing Public Resource Management Project (SERP, P177298) Project Implementation Unit (PIU). SoDMA provides coordination support. A Steering Committee chaired by the Director General of MoF provides strategic oversight, including representatives of key federal MDAs, FMS, and/or Benadir Regional Administration.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Steering Committee with federal MDAs and FMS representatives; coordination with SoDMA and National Emergency Operations Centre (NEOC)/Crisis Coordination and Response Hub (CCRH); pre-negotiated framework agreements with UN agencies and international NGO consortia; Somalia Food Security Crisis Preparedness Plan (FSCPP) quarterly meetings; Security Advisory Group at portfolio level.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Fast-track emergency procurement under Paragraph 12 of IPF Policy (Projects in Situations of Urgent Need of Assistance or Capacity Constraint). World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (5th Edition, September 2023). Direct selection of UN agencies via output agreements; direct or competitive selection of INGO consortia via implementing partner agreements. Pre-negotiated framework agreements with $0 initial allocation activated only upon eligible crisis. STEP used for tracking. CERP Manual governs procurement procedures.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: CERP PIU responsible for M&E and reporting. Results Framework guides progress monitoring during activation. Semi-annual reports during active crisis; annual process-focused reports when no crisis. Completion report 6 months after activation ends. Data collected from implementing partners and stakeholders. Gender-disaggregated indicators included. Implementation Completion and Results Report prepared if CERP activated at least once.

identified_risks.risk_description: Inherently high due to legacy of state fragmentation and civil war, incomplete constitutional review (especially allocation of powers between federal and state governments), and continued insurgency. Political contestation on core constitutional matters and resource sharing complicates institutional setup and service delivery.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Inclusion of affected Federal Member States in the project steering committee upon CERP activation; regular communication and inclusive, transparent decision-making processes.

identified_risks.risk_description: Low revenue mobilization (domestic revenue only 3% of GDP or $369 million in 2024, well below fragile state average of 11.5-12.5%), absence of discretion over spending, absence of monetary policy tools, heavy reliance on external financing in context of declining ODA. Emergency response costs estimated at $129 million/year against severely constrained fiscal space.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Anchoring project PIU in MoF to ensure CERP activation decisions are informed by deep understanding of competing priorities, fiscal pressures, and macroeconomic risks.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Public sector institutions remain underdeveloped due to legacy of state fragmentation, conflict, political divisions, minimal revenue mobilization, and inchoate policy consensus. Shortage of managerial and professional staff for emergency preparedness and response. Large volumes of humanitarian assistance channeled outside government systems.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Delegating implementation to reputable partners; establishing cross-sectoral coordination mechanisms; leveraging synergies with ongoing IPFs and ASAs; using USR for beneficiary identification to avoid parallel systems.

identified_risks.risk_description: Government financial and procurement controls remain weak; limited capacity to manage contracts; high levels of perceived corruption and elite capture.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Leveraging existing SERP PIU with proven track record; using reputable tested implementing partners; financial management controls with transparent procurement; regular audits and reporting; potential use of Hands-on Enhanced Implementation Support (HEIS) approach on procurement; direct Bank payments to implementing partners.

identified_risks.risk_description: Districts across Somalia experience violence and security incidents impacting access to locations and safety of staff and project stakeholders, complicating project implementation and monitoring. Continued Al-Shabaab insurgency and growing insecurity.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Coordination with implementing partners, security authorities, and local communities; remote monitoring tools; careful management of project visibility; Security Advisory Group at portfolio level; Somalia-based security risk management specialist hired to support PIUs.

identified_risks.risk_description: Gaps in DRM legislative framework; lack of consensus on hydromet agency responsibilities; limited coordination between federal and state levels on disaster management roles.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: CERP Manual updated regularly; alignment with FSCPP and existing policy frameworks; World Bank technical assistance through complementary projects.

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertainty about nature, timing, and scale of future crises; positive list must remain flexible and relevant.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: CERP Manual updated at least annually; positive list reviewed regularly; results framework updated during each activation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Emergency response activities carry moderate E&S risks; positive list designed to minimize physical infrastructure impacts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ESCP, ESMP, and SEP in place and disclosed; CERP Manual provides E&S compliance guidance; any ESCP changes require Bank agreement.

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Moderate (Environmental and Social Risk Classification: Moderate)
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes – Communities and individuals may submit complaints to existing SERP grievance mechanisms or the World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS). The Bank's independent Accountability Mechanism (AM) including the Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service is also available.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender analysis integrated; Somalia ranks 4th from last on Gender Inequality Index (0.776, 2022). M&E framework includes gender-disaggregated indicators: (a) beneficiaries receiving cash assistance of which are female (target: 51%), (b) beneficiaries of WASH services of which are women (target: 51%). Post-disaster recovery challenges for women and girls acknowledged alongside economic empowerment opportunities.
vulnerable_groups: Households affected by climate shocks (drought, flooding)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for a Fragile State and Conflict context (both boxes checked in implementation modalities). Conflict sensitivity addressed through: inclusion of FMS in steering committee to ensure inclusive decision-making; use of implementing partners with greater access in conflict-affected areas; careful management of project visibility to reduce security risks; coordination with security authorities; remote monitoring tools. Project recognizes that FCV dynamics interact with climate shocks creating a vicious cycle undermining government capacity. Government role in decision-making reinforced while leveraging partner access in insecure areas.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Somalia faces compounding fragility factors: (1) Legacy of civil war and state fragmentation; (2) Incomplete constitutional review and contested federal-state power allocation; (3) Continued Al-Shabaab insurgency; (4) Extremely low domestic revenue (3% of GDP); (5) Heavy dependence on humanitarian aid (>$1.1 billion/year average over 10 years) with declining ODA trends; (6) Climate vulnerability – one of world's most climate-vulnerable countries with recurrent droughts, floods, locust invasions; (7) Massive displacement – 2.3 million displaced by climate in 2023 vs 653,000 by conflict; (8) Weak public institutions and limited service delivery capacity; (9) Large humanitarian operations outside government systems; (10) Somalia only achieved HIPC Completion Point in December 2023, restoring IDA access.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risk rated High. Districts across Somalia experience violence and security incidents affecting access and staff safety. Continued insurgency (Al-Shabaab implied). Growing insecurity and economic uncertainty noted as context for maintaining implementing partner approach. Security managed through: Security Advisory Group at portfolio level; Somalia-based security risk management specialist; coordination with implementing partners, security authorities, and local communities; remote monitoring tools; careful project visibility management. African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia (AUSSOM) referenced in abbreviations.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes – significant displacement context. In 2023: 2.3 million Somalis displaced by climate shocks (1.7 million by flooding, 531,000 by drought) and 653,000 displaced by conflict and insecurity. Climate-related displacement on steep rise over past 8 years, affecting significantly more people than conflict. Displaced persons explicitly listed as target vulnerable group. Unified Social Registry used for beneficiary identification including displaced populations.
